neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate, rhythm, and prosody. No dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, able to spell WORLD backwards correctly. Recalls 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. Affect is mildly anxious but cooperative. No confusion noted; patient is able to give a clear and detailed account of the event.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam unremarkable, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact in all directions, no nystagmus, no ptosis. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in all three divisions bilaterally. Masseter and temporalis strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, no facial droop. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Weber midline, Rinne air > bone bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex present. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue protrudes midline, no fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in all four extremities, no rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups tested bilaterally: deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, finger abductors, iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstrings, tibialis anterior, gastrocnemius, and extensor hallucis longus. No pronator drift. No tremor.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, and vibration intact in all extremities. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. No dermatomal sensory loss.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus. No pathological reflexes.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin smooth and accurate bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal stride length and arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Mild diaphoresis noted on initial assessment, now resolved. Small area of urinary incontinence noted on arrival clothing. No tongue laceration or lateral tongue bite marks. Mild tenderness over left trapezius and rhomboid region with palpable trigger points. Skin pallor has resolved. No cyanosis. No external signs of head trauma.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden loss of consciousness and cardiac arrest during an outpatient procedure
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Jennifer Kowalski is a 38-year-old woman with a history of hyperlipidemia, generalized anxiety disorder, depression, chronic myofascial pain, and incidentally noted mitral valve prolapse who presents to the emergency department after experiencing sudden loss of consciousness and transient cardiac arrest during an outpatient ultrasound-guided trigger point injection procedure approximately 45 minutes ago. The patient had presented to a pain management clinic for scheduled trigger point injections of the left trapezius, levator scapulae, and rhomboid muscles using preservative-free 1% lidocaine without epinephrine. She reported anxiety about the procedure but agreed to proceed. She denied any allergies or prior adverse reactions to local anesthetics. Preprocedure vital signs were documented as stable with a blood pressure of 118/76 mmHg, heart rate of 74 bpm, and oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. Under sterile conditions and ultrasound guidance, a 27-gauge needle was advanced into the first trigger point in the left trapezius; aspiration was negative, and 1 mL of lidocaine was injected intramuscularly. The needle was then repositioned into an adjacent trigger point. Shortly after the second injection, the patient reported feeling dizzy and lightheaded. She was assisted from a seated position to lying on her right side. Within seconds she became unresponsive, appeared markedly pale, and was noted to have no palpable pulse. The treating physician performed a jaw thrust while staff retrieved an automated external defibrillator. The AED analyzed the rhythm and reported it as unshockable. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation was initiated immediately. After approximately 60 seconds of chest compressions, the patient regained consciousness and a palpable pulse returned. Upon regaining consciousness, she was noted to be diaphoretic and had experienced urinary incontinence. She was confused for approximately 20 to 30 seconds before becoming fully oriented. She denied any preceding chest pain, palpitations, or visual disturbances prior to the event. She also reports that over the past three months she has had two additional brief episodes of near-syncope, once while climbing a flight of stairs and once while carrying groceries, during which she felt an abrupt onset of lightheadedness and graying of vision lasting several seconds before spontaneously resolving. She did not seek medical attention for either of those episodes, attributing them to her anxiety. She denies any history of seizures, prior syncopal events before the past three months, or a known cardiac arrhythmia history.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and briefly drowsy EEG. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures captured during this 30-minute routine recording. Alternative non-epileptic etiologies for transient loss of consciousness should be strongly considered. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording. No events captured during the recording. Interictal sensitivity limited with a single routine EEG.

output.impression: Twenty-four-hour continuous cardiac monitoring demonstrates persistent complete (third-degree) atrioventricular block with a junctional escape rhythm averaging 40 bpm. The AV block is continuous and not paroxysmal, with no periods of normal AV conduction observed during the entire recording. Three significant pauses exceeding 2.5 seconds were documented, the longest at 3.8 seconds during sleep. There is chronotropic incompetence with the ventricular rate failing to exceed 48 bpm despite reported physical activity. No episodes of ventricular tachycardia, ventricular fibrillation, or sustained supraventricular arrhythmias. The persistent QTc prolongation is secondary to the marked bradycardia.
Abnormal values: Baseline supine heart rate 40 bpm bpm (H), 70-degree tilt heart rate at 5 min 42 bpm bpm (H).

findings.signal: cine: Normal wall motion in all segments; T1_mapping: Native T1 values within normal limits (no diffuse fibrosis); T2_mapping: No myocardial edema
pattern: Mid-wall linear enhancement in the basal interventricular septum; distribution: Non-ischemic pattern

output.impression: Cardiac MRI demonstrates normal biventricular volumes and systolic function. A small focus of mid-wall late gadolinium enhancement is identified in the basal interventricular septum in a non-ischemic pattern, likely representing focal fibrosis of the conduction system region. No evidence of myocarditis, sarcoidosis, or infiltrative cardiomyopathy. Mild mitral valve prolapse confirmed. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: cardiac syncope complete heart block young adults management

results.title: 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay
results.authors: Kusumoto FM, Schoenfeld MH, Barrett C, et al.
results.journal: Journal of the American College of Cardiology
results.summary: Class I recommendation for permanent pacemaker implantation in patients with third-degree AV block and symptomatic bradycardia. Complete heart block with syncope is an indication for permanent pacing regardless of the site of block. In younger patients without reversible cause, dual-chamber pacing (DDD) is recommended.

results.title: Causes and Clinical Significance of Complete Atrioventricular Block in Adults Under 40 Years
results.authors: Michaelsson M, Jonzon A, Riesenfeld T
results.summary: Complete AV block in young adults is most commonly congenital or related to idiopathic fibrosis of the conduction system (Lenegre disease). Acquired causes include myocarditis, Lyme disease, cardiac sarcoidosis, and infiltrative cardiomyopathies. All symptomatic patients require permanent pacemaker implantation. Screening for associated structural heart disease with echocardiography and cardiac MRI is recommended.

results.title: Differentiating Cardiac Syncope from Epileptic Seizures: Clinical Features and Diagnostic Approach
results.authors: Sheldon R, Rose S, Ritchie D, et al.
results.summary: Convulsive syncope (myoclonic jerking during syncope) occurs in up to 12% of cardiac syncope cases and can be mistaken for seizures. Key distinguishing features include: absence of tonic phase, very brief duration (<15 seconds) of convulsive movements, rapid return to baseline consciousness, absence of post-ictal confusion, and abnormal ECG. Normal EEG supports cardiac rather than epileptic etiology.

output.summary: Complete heart block is a well-established cause of cardiac syncope. In patients under 40, the most common etiologies are congenital AV block and idiopathic progressive fibrosis of the conduction system (Lenegre disease). Current ACC/AHA/HRS guidelines provide a Class I (strongest) recommendation for permanent pacemaker implantation in symptomatic complete heart block. The differentiation from epileptic seizures is important, as convulsive syncope with myoclonic movements can mimic seizures. A normal EEG combined with an abnormal ECG showing high-grade conduction disease strongly supports a cardiac etiology.

interactions: Escitalopram can prolong the QTc interval in a dose-dependent manner
In the setting of pre-existing QTc prolongation (492 ms on current ECG), escitalopram may further increase the risk of torsades de pointes
contraindications: Caution advised with QTc >500 ms; current QTc is 492 ms, which is borderline
warnings: Monitor QTc closely after pacemaker implantation when heart rate normalizes
The current QTc prolongation is likely primarily due to bradycardia and may normalize once heart rate is restored with pacing
If QTc remains prolonged after adequate pacing, consider switching to an SSRI with less QTc prolongation risk (e.g., sertraline)
alternatives: Cognitive behavioral therapy for anxiety management

output.query: local anesthetic systemic toxicity lidocaine cardiac arrest trigger point injection

results.title: Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity: Current and Emerging Therapies
results.summary: LAST typically occurs with inadvertent intravascular injection or absorption of large doses. The toxic threshold for lidocaine is 4.5 mg/kg without epinephrine. CNS symptoms (perioral numbness, tinnitus, seizures) typically precede cardiovascular toxicity. A dose of 1-2 mL of 1% lidocaine (10-20 mg total) is far below the toxic threshold for any adult patient and is exceedingly unlikely to cause systemic toxicity.

results.title: Cardiac Arrest During Trigger Point Injections: A Case Series and Review
results.summary: Most cases of cardiac arrest during trigger point injections are due to vasovagal responses triggered by pain, anxiety, and needle phobia. When cardiac arrest occurs with very low doses of local anesthetic administered via proper intramuscular technique under ultrasound guidance, LAST is exceedingly unlikely. Pre-existing cardiac conduction disease should be considered in patients who experience cardiac arrest during minor procedures.

output.summary: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) is extremely unlikely with the low dose of lidocaine used in this case (10-20 mg total, far below the 4.5 mg/kg toxic threshold). Cardiac arrest during trigger point injections is most commonly attributed to vasovagal syncope, but in patients with underlying conduction disease, the procedure-related vagal stimulation from pain and anxiety can unmask or worsen pre-existing AV block, precipitating hemodynamically significant bradycardia or asystole.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to complete (third-degree) atrioventricular block

differential.key_distinguishing: Procedural setting with pain and anxiety could suggest vasovagal etiology, but the complete heart block on ECG, prior near-syncopal episodes unrelated to pain or emotional triggers, and chronotropic incompetence establish a cardiac etiology as primary

The total lidocaine dose (10-20 mg) was far below toxic thresholds; proper intramuscular placement was confirmed with ultrasound; no preceding CNS symptoms of LAST (tinnitus, perioral numbness, seizure)

Normal EEG without epileptiform discharges, very brief loss of consciousness with rapid return to baseline, no tonic-clonic activity, no post-ictal confusion beyond 20-30 seconds, and ECG showing complete heart block
No urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, or other allergic features; true IgE-mediated lidocaine allergy is exceedingly rare; rapid resolution inconsistent with anaphylaxis

Although patient has anxiety and depression, the documented loss of pulse and unshockable rhythm on AED with CPR requirement cannot be psychogenic; complete heart block on ECG provides a clear organic explanation

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately upon arrival to emergency department
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Complete (third-degree) AV block with junctional escape rhythm at 38 bpm

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory evaluation including CBC, BMP, cardiac biomarkers (troponin, BNP), TSH, and magnesium
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs, ruling out metabolic and ischemic causes of conduction block

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptic etiology given witnessed loss of consciousness with possible convulsive movements
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG without epileptiform discharges, supporting cardiac rather than epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Request urgent cardiology consultation for permanent pacemaker implantation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Cardiology concurs with indication for permanent dual-chamber pacemaker

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous telemetry monitoring and transcutaneous pacing capability at bedside
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Continuous monitoring for hemodynamic stability while awaiting pacemaker

optimal_actions.action: Order transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate for structural heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal biventricular function, mild MVP, no structural cause of AV block

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on cardiac syncope and complete heart block management in young adults
optimal_actions.expected_finding: ACC/AHA guidelines confirm Class I indication for permanent pacing in symptomatic complete heart block

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for current medications in context of QTc prolongation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Escitalopram may contribute to QTc prolongation; monitor after pacemaker implantation

optimal_actions.action: Order Lyme serology, inflammatory markers, and ACE level to exclude reversible causes of AV block
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative Lyme serology, normal inflammatory markers, excluding reversible infectious or inflammatory causes

critical_actions: Identify complete heart block on ECG as the cause of syncope and cardiac arrest
Initiate continuous cardiac monitoring with transcutaneous pacing capability immediately available
Obtain urgent cardiology consultation for permanent pacemaker implantation
Rule out reversible causes of complete heart block (electrolytes, ischemia, Lyme disease, hypothyroidism)
Recognize that the procedural setting (trigger point injection) was a coincidental context and not the primary cause of the cardiac arrest
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing the event as vasovagal syncope without recognizing the underlying complete heart block
Initiating antiepileptic medications based on the brief convulsive movements (convulsive syncope, not seizure)
Discharging the patient without continuous cardiac monitoring and cardiology evaluation
Attributing the cardiac arrest solely to local anesthetic toxicity and missing the underlying conduction disease
Administering atropine as definitive treatment for complete heart block (may be temporarily useful but does not address the need for permanent pacing)
Starting rate-lowering medications (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers) which would worsen bradycardia
key_reasoning_points: The complete heart block on ECG is the unifying diagnosis explaining both the acute cardiac arrest during the procedure and the prior near-syncopal episodes over the past three months

Vasovagal syncope was a reasonable initial consideration given the procedural context (pain, anxiety, needle phobia) but the ECG findings and history of prior unprovoked near-syncopal episodes point to a cardiac conduction etiology

Local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) is exceedingly unlikely given the very low dose of lidocaine (10-20 mg total), proper intramuscular placement confirmed by ultrasound, and absence of preceding CNS toxicity symptoms

The brief convulsive movements observed represent convulsive syncope (myoclonic jerking due to cerebral hypoperfusion) rather than an epileptic seizure, as supported by the normal EEG and rapid return to baseline consciousness
Normal cardiac biomarkers (troponin, BNP) exclude acute myocardial infarction and decompensated heart failure as contributors

The family history of sudden cardiac death in the father at age 45 and paternal grandmother requiring a pacemaker raises concern for a familial conduction system disease and should prompt genetic counseling consideration
The QTc prolongation observed is secondary to the marked bradycardia and is expected to improve with adequate pacing; however, the concurrent use of escitalopram warrants monitoring

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case: Complete heart block is clearly visible on the initial ECG, providing unambiguous evidence for a cardiac etiology. The history of prior near-syncopal episodes further supports the diagnosis. Minimal confounders are present, though the procedural setting could initially misdirect toward vasovagal syncope or LAST.
